full_ccd.situation: Alex's cousin invited him to attend his upcoming wedding.
full_ccd.automatic_thoughts: It will be stressful and negative; people will ask me questions I cannot answer or don't want to answer; my mum would be ashamed of me and critical of me as always; They don't want me there anyway, nobody likes me in this family.
full_ccd.emotions: anxious, worried, fearful, scared, tense; sad, down, lonely, unhappy
full_ccd.behavior: Ignored the invitation and did not respond to the RSVP request. Ignored phone calls from family.
full_ccd.intermediate_beliefs:
Helpless: I'm just not very good at handling stress and I have poor self-control, which is why I need to not put myself in stressful situations. 
 Unlovable: If I show my true self, people will reject me the way my mother rejected me.
full_ccd.coping_strategies: The patient has adopted avoidance as a coping strategy by distancing himself from his family to reduce exposure to negativity and conflict. He has started scheduling pleasant activities and planning his day ahead of time in order to maintain a sense of control over his emotions and circumstances.
full_ccd.core_beliefs:
Helpless: I am trapped.; I am out of control.
Unlovable: I am unlovable.; I am undesirable, unwanted.
full_ccd.relevant_history: The patient has a history of substance abuse and has been through rehab to overcome it. He has had issues with his family, particularly with his mother, where he has felt rejected and emotionally neglected. He has struggled with obesity since childhood, which has affected his self-esteem and body image. He also has a history of being victimized and bullied due to his weight.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text:
Uh, yeah, I mean, it’s, uh, it’s hard to say. I guess there’s a lot on my mind, but also, like, nothing at the same time? You know how it is, right? It’s just, um, everything feels jumbled up in there. I’ve been thinking about, um, stuff from my past, but I don’t really know why. Like, I keep going back to situations that, I mean, they don’t really make sense. 

And then I get all worked up about things that happened, like, years ago, and it feels stupid, but I can’t help it. I mean, why does that happen? Why can’t I just, you know, move on or whatever? It’s like I’m stuck in a loop, and I’m just tired of it, honestly. But, I don’t know, maybe it’s not even that important? I guess I’m just rambling now. What do you think?

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you’re experiencing a lot of mixed feelings about your past, and it’s understandable to feel stuck when those memories keep resurfacing. It’s not unusual to find ourselves in that loop, even if it feels frustrating or silly. When you say it feels like you’re rambling, I hear a sense of exhaustion around these thoughts. Can you tell me a bit more about what specific memories or situations from the past you keep returning to?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text:
Client: Well, yeah, I mean, I guess there's a few that stick out, but it's like, um, where do I even start? I don’t know. Like, um, sometimes I think about, uh, my childhood, and it just... it feels like, I dunno, a lifetime ago, but still so close? It's weird. I was always the bigger kid, you know? And, um, that brought a lot of attention, but not the good kind. I mean, I got bullied a lot, and I think about those kids sometimes, how they’d, I don’t know, just target me for no reason. I mean, it’s not like I asked to be different or anything, right? 

And then there’s my mom. I guess I think about our relationship a lot too. It’s complicated. I can’t help but feel like she didn’t really see me, you know? Like, I was just... there, but not really there. I don’t want to sound like I’m blaming her or anything. It’s just, um, I don’t know. I feel like I’m always trying to prove something, but I don’t even know to whom. It’s like I’m running in circles. But, uh, I mean, it’s not like anyone else cares about that stuff, right? It’s just me being... whatever. I guess I’m just... I don’t know.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of weight from your childhood experiences, especially regarding the bullying and your relationship with your mom. It’s understandable to feel like those memories still hold a strong presence in your life. When you mention trying to prove something, it makes me wonder what that means for you. What do you think you're trying to prove, and to whom do you feel that pressure?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text:
Client: Uh, yeah, I mean, that's a tough one, right? I guess I don't really know exactly who I'm trying to prove anything to. It's like, um, I feel this pressure, but it kind of feels like it’s coming from everywhere and nowhere at the same time, you know? Like, maybe I’m trying to prove to myself that I’m worth something, or that I’m not just that kid who got picked on. But then again, it’s like, who am I trying to impress? 

I mean, I don’t know, I want to be seen, to be, I guess, valued? But I feel like I’m always... I don’t know, falling short or something. Like, it’s never enough. I think about my mom and how she never really gave me that kind of validation, and then I just... I'm left trying to fill that void or whatever. It’s exhausting. It’s like I’m stuck in this constant battle inside my head. And then, sometimes I wonder if I’m just making it all up, you know? Like, am I even allowed to feel this way? Or am I just... overreacting? I don’t know. It's just frustrating, honestly.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you’re navigating a really complex mix of feelings around self-worth and validation, especially in relation to your childhood experiences and your mom. I hear a sense of exhaustion and frustration in trying to prove your value, yet feeling like it’s a never-ending battle. It’s completely valid to feel this way, and it’s understandable to question whether your feelings are justified. When you think about wanting to be seen and valued, what does that look like for you? What would it mean to you to feel that sense of validation?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text:
Client: Uh, yeah, I mean, that’s another tough one, right? What does it mean to feel seen? I guess, um, I’ve thought about that a lot, too. It’s like, you know, I want people to recognize me for who I really am, not just the surface stuff. Like, I don’t want to be just that guy who, I dunno, struggled with weight or had a rough childhood. I want them to see, um, the real me, you know? 

But then I start to wonder, like, if they really saw me, would they even like me? Would they care? It’s a scary thought because, I mean, I’ve been rejected before, and it really stings. So, I guess I just want, um, I don’t know, acceptance? Maybe it’s just about finding a place where I belong, where I don’t feel like I have to put on a show or anything. 

But, um, I don’t know if I even know what that looks like. I mean, sometimes it feels like I’m chasing this idea of validation, but I’m just running in circles again. And then I get all tangled up in my head, wondering if I’m worthy of it in the first place? It’s frustrating because I feel like, um, I’m always falling short somehow, and I don’t know how to break that cycle. It’s just... heavy, you know?

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It seems like you’re grappling with a deep desire for acceptance and understanding, along with the fear that if people truly saw you, they might not like what they find. That sounds incredibly heavy to carry. The idea of being recognized for who you really are, beyond past struggles, feels important, yet it also brings up a lot of uncertainty for you. When you think about the cycle of feeling unworthy or falling short, what do those thoughts sound like in your head? How do they affect how you see yourself?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text:
Client: Uh, yeah, I mean, those thoughts, they’re pretty loud, honestly. It’s like this constant, um, inner dialogue that just won’t quit. I guess it goes something like, um, “You’ll never be good enough,” or “Who do you think you are?” It’s like, I can hear that voice in my head, and it feels, I don’t know, suffocating? Like, no matter what I do, it’s never enough to drown it out. 

And then I start thinking about, like, all the times I’ve put myself out there, trying to be, I don’t know, confident or whatever, and it just... it always backfires. I mean, I can’t help but remember those moments when I got shut down or ignored, and it’s like, um, I just want to scream, you know? It feels like I’m stuck in this loop of trying to prove myself, but the harder I try, the more I feel like I’m failing. 

And it’s just so exhausting because, like, I’m constantly second-guessing myself. Am I being too much? Am I being too little? It’s like I can’t find that balance. I want to be seen, but at the same time, I’m terrified of being rejected again. I don’t know if that makes sense, but it’s just this heavy weight that keeps dragging me down, and I don’t know how to shake it off. I guess I just feel... trapped, you know?

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you’re feeling really overwhelmed by that persistent inner dialogue, and it’s understandably suffocating to hear those critical thoughts. The tension between wanting to be seen and the fear of rejection seems to create a heavy cycle of self-doubt for you. It’s clear that the memories of being shut down or ignored have a lasting impact on how you view yourself now. When you describe feeling trapped, I wonder if there are moments or situations where you feel a little more at ease with yourself, even if they’re fleeting. Can you think of any?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text:
Client: Uh, yeah, I mean... that’s a tough question. I guess, um, there are maybe a few moments, but they’re kind of rare, you know? Like, maybe when I’m, um, doing something I really enjoy, like, uh, cooking or playing video games. Sometimes, when I’m really into it, I can kind of forget about all that noise in my head for a little while. It’s like I lose myself in it, and, um, nothing else matters at that moment. 

But then, of course, it doesn’t last long, right? Like, once I step away, it’s like I’m right back in it again, and I start overthinking everything. It’s frustrating because I want to hold on to that feeling of ease, but it just slips away so quickly. And then I think, like, well, is it even enough? I mean, I want to feel that way more often, but it’s just... hard, you know? 

Sometimes, I wonder if it’s just a distraction, like I’m using those things to avoid dealing with all the other stuff. And then I feel guilty for not, I don’t know, facing my problems head-on? It's like I can’t win either way. I just feel... stuck, I guess. Ugh. It’s all just so complicated.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It seems like those moments of enjoyment, like when you’re cooking or playing video games, provide a temporary escape from the heaviness of your thoughts, which is completely understandable. It’s great that you have found things that give you some relief, even if it feels fleeting. I hear a sense of guilt in your words about using those activities as distractions, and I wonder if it might be helpful to explore what it is about those moments that feels good to you. What do you think it is that makes you feel more at ease in those times?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text:
Client: Uh, yeah, I mean, that’s a good question, right? I guess, um, when I'm cooking or playing video games, it’s like I can just focus on the task at hand, you know? Like, there’s this kind of, um, flow to it, where I’m not thinking about all the stuff that usually weighs me down. When I’m cooking, I get to be creative, and it’s like I’m pouring my energy into something tangible, something that I can see and taste, and it feels rewarding. 

And with video games, I can just, um, escape into a different world, where I can be anyone but me, you know? It’s less about proving anything and more about just having fun, even if it's, um, just for a little while. But then there's that nagging voice in the back of my head, like, “You should be doing something more productive,” or “Is this really worth your time?” It’s frustrating because I want to enjoy those moments, but I can’t help but feel guilty afterward. 

And I think maybe part of me wishes I could just, I don’t know, be okay with it? Like, why can’t I just let myself have those moments without worrying about the fallout? It’s like I’m always waiting for the other shoe to drop or something, and I don’t know how to break that cycle of guilt. I just want to feel... I don’t know, free? But it feels so complicated, like there’s always something holding me back.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like those activities bring you a sense of creativity and escape, allowing you to tap into a rewarding experience, even if just for a moment. I can hear how frustrating it is to have that nagging voice undermining your enjoyment and adding guilt to something that should feel freeing. It’s understandable to want to embrace those moments without the weight of self-criticism. When you think about that desire to feel free and just enjoy those experiences, what do you think might help you shift that mindset and allow yourself to fully embrace those moments without the guilt?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text:
Client: Uh, yeah, I mean, that’s a tough one. I guess, um, part of me thinks maybe I need to just, I don’t know, let go of that voice? Like, stop trying to judge everything so harshly? But it’s easier said than done, right? I mean, I’ve been doing that for so long, just, um, critiquing myself every step of the way. It’s like I’ve built up this habit of, I don’t know, feeling guilty about enjoying things, and it’s hard to break free from that.

But then again, I wonder if I could just remind myself that it’s okay to take a break, you know? I mean, everyone needs downtime, right? It’s just... I think I get so caught up in thinking I should be doing more or being more productive that I forget to just be. I mean, it’s like I’m always chasing this idea of doing something worthwhile, but maybe, um, enjoying the moment is worthwhile too? 

But then, of course, there’s that fear that if I let myself feel that way, I’ll just, I don’t know, spiral back into not doing anything at all, you know? It’s a mixed bag of wanting to enjoy life but being terrified of what happens if I relax too much. So, I guess, I’m just trying to figure out how to find that balance without feeling like I’m, I don’t know, falling apart or something. It’s just... all so complicated, I guess.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you’re really reflecting on the balance between allowing yourself to enjoy life and the fear of losing control if you let go of that critical voice. It’s understandable to feel caught in that tension, especially after so long. You mentioned wanting to remind yourself that it’s okay to take a break and that enjoying the moment can be worthwhile too. When you think about gradually letting go of that harsh self-judgment, what might be a small step you could take to start practicing that acceptance for yourself?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text:
Client: Uh, yeah, I mean, that’s a good question, right? Small steps, sure. I guess maybe I could, I don’t know, try to set aside some time just for those activities, like cooking or gaming, and tell myself it’s okay to just enjoy it without all that pressure? Like, um, maybe I could start with, I don’t know, scheduling a specific time, like, on a weekend or something, just to dive into it without feeling like I should be doing something else? 

But then I start to worry, like, what if I can’t stick to it? What if I end up feeling guilty again? It’s like I want to give myself that space, but it feels risky, you know? I mean, what if I really do just spiral and end up wasting time instead of, I don’t know, making progress? Ugh, it’s just so hard to shake off that guilt. 

But I guess, um, if I could remind myself that it’s okay to take a break, maybe that would help? I could try to practice just being in those moments, even if it’s uncomfortable at first. I don’t know, it feels like a lot, but maybe it’s worth a shot? I just... I don't know, I want to believe I can find that balance, but it’s like I’m stuck in my own head again, you know?

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you’re really considering how to create that space for enjoyment, which is a great step forward. I hear the mix of hope and uncertainty in your thoughts about setting aside time for yourself. It’s understandable to feel hesitant about whether you can stick to it or if guilt might creep back in. You mentioned wanting to practice being in those moments, even if it feels uncomfortable at first. What do you think might help you stay grounded in that practice, especially when the guilt starts to surface?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text:
Client: Uh, yeah, I mean, staying grounded, that’s, um, definitely a challenge, right? I guess, um, maybe it could help if I, I don’t know, set some kind of reminder for myself? Like, I could write down a few affirmations or something that remind me it’s okay to enjoy those moments, you know? Just something simple like, “It’s okay to take a break” or “You deserve this time.” 

But then, I think about how I might just, I don’t know, read them and not really feel them, if that makes sense? Like, I could say those things, but my brain might still be like, “Yeah, but you should be doing something more important.” It’s frustrating because I want to believe those affirmations, but it feels like there’s this wall I have to break through. 

And then maybe I could, um, start journaling about how I feel during those moments when I do allow myself to enjoy things? Like, just writing down what I felt, what I liked about it, and try to focus on the positive instead of letting that guilt take over. I think that might help me see those moments more clearly, you know? But, uh, it’s still a bit intimidating. I just, I don’t know, want to make sure I don’t end up caught in my head again. It just feels like, I don’t know, a lot to juggle, you know?

turns.therapist: I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

turns.client:
Client: Uh, yeah, I mean, that’s another tough one, right? What does it mean to feel seen? I guess, um, I’ve thought about that a lot, too. It’s like, you know, I want people to recognize me for who I really am, not just the surface stuff. Like, I don’t want to be just that guy who, I dunno, struggled with weight or had a rough childhood. I want them to see, um, the real me, you know? 

But then I start to wonder, like, if they really saw me, would they even like me? Would they care? It’s a scary thought because, I mean, I’ve been rejected before, and it really stings. So, I guess I just want, um, I don’t know, acceptance? Maybe it’s just about finding a place where I belong, where I don’t feel like I have to put on a show or anything. 

But, um, I don’t know if I even know what that looks like. I mean, sometimes it feels like I’m chasing this idea of validation, but I’m just running in circles again. And then I get all tangled up in my head, wondering if I’m worthy of it in the first place? It’s frustrating because I feel like, um, I’m always falling short somehow, and I don’t know how to break that cycle. It’s just... heavy, you know?

turns.therapist: It seems like you’re grappling with a deep desire for acceptance and understanding, along with the fear that if people truly saw you, they might not like what they find. That sounds incredibly heavy to carry. The idea of being recognized for who you really are, beyond past struggles, feels important, yet it also brings up a lot of uncertainty for you. When you think about the cycle of feeling unworthy or falling short, what do those thoughts sound like in your head? How do they affect how you see yourself?

turns.client:
Client: Uh, yeah, I mean, those thoughts, they’re pretty loud, honestly. It’s like this constant, um, inner dialogue that just won’t quit. I guess it goes something like, um, “You’ll never be good enough,” or “Who do you think you are?” It’s like, I can hear that voice in my head, and it feels, I don’t know, suffocating? Like, no matter what I do, it’s never enough to drown it out. 

And then I start thinking about, like, all the times I’ve put myself out there, trying to be, I don’t know, confident or whatever, and it just... it always backfires. I mean, I can’t help but remember those moments when I got shut down or ignored, and it’s like, um, I just want to scream, you know? It feels like I’m stuck in this loop of trying to prove myself, but the harder I try, the more I feel like I’m failing. 

And it’s just so exhausting because, like, I’m constantly second-guessing myself. Am I being too much? Am I being too little? It’s like I can’t find that balance. I want to be seen, but at the same time, I’m terrified of being rejected again. I don’t know if that makes sense, but it’s just this heavy weight that keeps dragging me down, and I don’t know how to shake it off. I guess I just feel... trapped, you know?

turns.therapist: It sounds like you’re feeling really overwhelmed by that persistent inner dialogue, and it’s understandably suffocating to hear those critical thoughts. The tension between wanting to be seen and the fear of rejection seems to create a heavy cycle of self-doubt for you. It’s clear that the memories of being shut down or ignored have a lasting impact on how you view yourself now. When you describe feeling trapped, I wonder if there are moments or situations where you feel a little more at ease with yourself, even if they’re fleeting. Can you think of any?

turns.client:
Client: Uh, yeah, I mean... that’s a tough question. I guess, um, there are maybe a few moments, but they’re kind of rare, you know? Like, maybe when I’m, um, doing something I really enjoy, like, uh, cooking or playing video games. Sometimes, when I’m really into it, I can kind of forget about all that noise in my head for a little while. It’s like I lose myself in it, and, um, nothing else matters at that moment. 

But then, of course, it doesn’t last long, right? Like, once I step away, it’s like I’m right back in it again, and I start overthinking everything. It’s frustrating because I want to hold on to that feeling of ease, but it just slips away so quickly. And then I think, like, well, is it even enough? I mean, I want to feel that way more often, but it’s just... hard, you know? 

Sometimes, I wonder if it’s just a distraction, like I’m using those things to avoid dealing with all the other stuff. And then I feel guilty for not, I don’t know, facing my problems head-on? It's like I can’t win either way. I just feel... stuck, I guess. Ugh. It’s all just so complicated.

turns.therapist: It seems like those moments of enjoyment, like when you’re cooking or playing video games, provide a temporary escape from the heaviness of your thoughts, which is completely understandable. It’s great that you have found things that give you some relief, even if it feels fleeting. I hear a sense of guilt in your words about using those activities as distractions, and I wonder if it might be helpful to explore what it is about those moments that feels good to you. What do you think it is that makes you feel more at ease in those times?

turns.client:
Client: Uh, yeah, I mean, that’s a good question, right? I guess, um, when I'm cooking or playing video games, it’s like I can just focus on the task at hand, you know? Like, there’s this kind of, um, flow to it, where I’m not thinking about all the stuff that usually weighs me down. When I’m cooking, I get to be creative, and it’s like I’m pouring my energy into something tangible, something that I can see and taste, and it feels rewarding. 

And with video games, I can just, um, escape into a different world, where I can be anyone but me, you know? It’s less about proving anything and more about just having fun, even if it's, um, just for a little while. But then there's that nagging voice in the back of my head, like, “You should be doing something more productive,” or “Is this really worth your time?” It’s frustrating because I want to enjoy those moments, but I can’t help but feel guilty afterward. 

And I think maybe part of me wishes I could just, I don’t know, be okay with it? Like, why can’t I just let myself have those moments without worrying about the fallout? It’s like I’m always waiting for the other shoe to drop or something, and I don’t know how to break that cycle of guilt. I just want to feel... I don’t know, free? But it feels so complicated, like there’s always something holding me back.

turns.therapist: It sounds like those activities bring you a sense of creativity and escape, allowing you to tap into a rewarding experience, even if just for a moment. I can hear how frustrating it is to have that nagging voice undermining your enjoyment and adding guilt to something that should feel freeing. It’s understandable to want to embrace those moments without the weight of self-criticism. When you think about that desire to feel free and just enjoy those experiences, what do you think might help you shift that mindset and allow yourself to fully embrace those moments without the guilt?

turns.client:
Client: Uh, yeah, I mean, that’s a tough one. I guess, um, part of me thinks maybe I need to just, I don’t know, let go of that voice? Like, stop trying to judge everything so harshly? But it’s easier said than done, right? I mean, I’ve been doing that for so long, just, um, critiquing myself every step of the way. It’s like I’ve built up this habit of, I don’t know, feeling guilty about enjoying things, and it’s hard to break free from that.

But then again, I wonder if I could just remind myself that it’s okay to take a break, you know? I mean, everyone needs downtime, right? It’s just... I think I get so caught up in thinking I should be doing more or being more productive that I forget to just be. I mean, it’s like I’m always chasing this idea of doing something worthwhile, but maybe, um, enjoying the moment is worthwhile too? 

But then, of course, there’s that fear that if I let myself feel that way, I’ll just, I don’t know, spiral back into not doing anything at all, you know? It’s a mixed bag of wanting to enjoy life but being terrified of what happens if I relax too much. So, I guess, I’m just trying to figure out how to find that balance without feeling like I’m, I don’t know, falling apart or something. It’s just... all so complicated, I guess.

turns.therapist: It sounds like you’re really reflecting on the balance between allowing yourself to enjoy life and the fear of losing control if you let go of that critical voice. It’s understandable to feel caught in that tension, especially after so long. You mentioned wanting to remind yourself that it’s okay to take a break and that enjoying the moment can be worthwhile too. When you think about gradually letting go of that harsh self-judgment, what might be a small step you could take to start practicing that acceptance for yourself?

turns.client:
Client: Uh, yeah, I mean, that’s a good question, right? Small steps, sure. I guess maybe I could, I don’t know, try to set aside some time just for those activities, like cooking or gaming, and tell myself it’s okay to just enjoy it without all that pressure? Like, um, maybe I could start with, I don’t know, scheduling a specific time, like, on a weekend or something, just to dive into it without feeling like I should be doing something else? 

But then I start to worry, like, what if I can’t stick to it? What if I end up feeling guilty again? It’s like I want to give myself that space, but it feels risky, you know? I mean, what if I really do just spiral and end up wasting time instead of, I don’t know, making progress? Ugh, it’s just so hard to shake off that guilt. 

But I guess, um, if I could remind myself that it’s okay to take a break, maybe that would help? I could try to practice just being in those moments, even if it’s uncomfortable at first. I don’t know, it feels like a lot, but maybe it’s worth a shot? I just... I don't know, I want to believe I can find that balance, but it’s like I’m stuck in my own head again, you know?

turns.therapist: It sounds like you’re really considering how to create that space for enjoyment, which is a great step forward. I hear the mix of hope and uncertainty in your thoughts about setting aside time for yourself. It’s understandable to feel hesitant about whether you can stick to it or if guilt might creep back in. You mentioned wanting to practice being in those moments, even if it feels uncomfortable at first. What do you think might help you stay grounded in that practice, especially when the guilt starts to surface?

turns.client:
Client: Uh, yeah, I mean, staying grounded, that’s, um, definitely a challenge, right? I guess, um, maybe it could help if I, I don’t know, set some kind of reminder for myself? Like, I could write down a few affirmations or something that remind me it’s okay to enjoy those moments, you know? But then, I think about how I might just, I don’t know, read them and not really feel them, if that makes sense? Like, I could say those things, but my brain might still be like, “Yeah, but you should be doing something more important.” It’s frustrating because I want to believe those affirmations, but it feels like there’s this wall I have to break through. 

And then maybe I could, um, start journaling about how I feel during those moments when I do allow myself to enjoy things? Like, just writing down what I felt, what I liked about it, and try to focus on the positive instead of letting that guilt take over. I think that might help me see those moments more clearly, you know? But, uh, it’s still a bit intimidating. I just, I don’t know, want to make sure I don’t end up caught in my head again. It just feels like, I don’t know, a lot to juggle, you know?
